title: 2025 08 13 - HE
displayTitle: 2025-08-13 - Health Committee - Briefing from Michael Papesch
description: 13 August 2025 | Health Committee Briefing from Michael Papesch Health New Zealand—Te Whatu Ora Dame Helen Stokes-Lampard, National Chief Medical Officer Rachel Haggerty, Director, Funding - Hospitals, Planning, Funding and Outcomes Petition of Kathleen Walsh: Urgently find obstetric specialists for maternity services at Whakatāne Hospital Kathleen Walsh Petition of Fluoride Free New Zealand: Stop water fluoridation Fluoride Free New Zealand Mary Byrne Mark Atkin Briefing from Bronda Smith Royal New Zealand College of General Practitioners Dr Luke Bradford, Medical Director Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Providing equitable access to planned care treatment Office of the Auditor-General Leeanne McAviney, Assistant Auditor-General Karen Wong, Manager Sector Performance Richard Towers, Principal Performance Auditor
descriptionSummary: Briefing from Michael Papesch. Health New Zealand—Te Whatu Ora. Dame Helen Stokes-Lampard, National Chief Medical Officer

chunks.text: I have a cup of
Good morning.

Well, welcome here. Health New Zealand. I'm Sam Dale, chair of the health committee. You'll state. Great. Uh, you'll see members around the table as well. Uh, I'll give you a moment to introduce yourself, and then, um, we'll kick it off. I know members will have questions for you. Thank you. Come back. Helen Stokes Lahar.

I am Helen Stokes Lahar, the National Chief Clinical Medical Officer for Health of New Zealand. Um, I'll just leave it at that. Uh, Koto. I'm Rachel Haggerty. I am the director of HSS funding, so hospital specialist services funding for Health New Zealand. Excellent. Thank you. All right.

Um, I think I'll go straight to, I'll give you a couple of minutes to, to say a few words and then we'll go to member's questions. Thank you. Yeah. Perfect. Thank you. Um, we didn't, certainly didn't prepare lengthy opening comments because we wanted to know what was an interest to you. Thank you. Perhaps just a few brief ones to pick up some of the issues that we have been made aware of informally, uh, about this process.

Um, I guess want to start by saying there is absolutely no dispute that organ donation is something that every employee of Health New Zealand is fully supportive of. Um, it's vital importance to the country and to our people, but at the heart of all categoric donation is absolute tragedy. And, uh, the unexpected death of an otherwise healthy person, it means that there is a huge emotional toll on families, um, and of course on the staff involved in the process.

Um, but organ donation is a core part of our business, as usual as an organization. And, uh, we're really proud of the achievements of our clinicians and their collaborations. Uh, despite the global decline in donation rates, post pandemic, um, there've been questions about ICU bed capacity, but we've actually had a very positive uplift in I intensive care beds in the recent years.

Um, and I want to be clear that ITU or ICU bed capacity is not, um, a barrier to undertaking deceased organ, organ, uh, tissue and tissue donation. Uh, the health landscape's obviously changed in recent years since the formation of Health New Zealand. Um, and as a delivery organization, health New Zealand is focused on our improvement efforts are all focused on set of strategic priorities, obviously set by the ministry, um, and health targets.

Um, with respect to the NZ bar, so New Zealand blood and organ service strategy is, they obviously the, obviously delivery of the strategy, but we work and collaborate very effectively with them. And who Implements the strategy? Sorry, The, the strategy sits with New Zealand Blood and Organ Services. Yes. Um, we are, we on the delivery arm, we, our role is in consenting donors and then separately undertaking transplantations, two very separate exercises that have to be kept separate, and we've got very constructive working relationships.

I know Sarah Morley well, she, and I've met like months ago. I went out to visit the headquarters in Auckland, which is a very positive, and she meets with my Chief Medical officers. Um, there's been questions about link funding for nurses and obviously Rachel can expand, but the money for Link nurses just absorbed into our baseline organization. And this is business as usual.

There's no question about ongoing funding of that. It is BAU for Health New Zealand. Uh, it would be great if even more of our staff could be trained to have the difficult conversations, and that's something we can always come back to, but it's because it's such hard work to do. Well, my clinical background, I'm GP and I know, uh, up close and personal, the impact on families, but actually the positive effect on families subsequently in the grieving process to know that out to the worst tragedy, something good has happened.

Um, I mentioned ITU bed capacity, I can give you more detail on that if you want it, but we, we are committed to raising this with our staff, and I've used this as a lever to open up some really good conversations with my senior ICU colleagues. Um, and there is continuous improvement, ongoing and collaboration.

And I'm delighted with the recent signing of the MOU with our colleagues in Australia about the organ match process. Not sure if that's come to your attention, but that's a really positive example of where we can give you better and well for our people here and overseas. So I'll probably stop there, but thank you. Okay, thank you. Alright. Um, we, we did hear some concerns that the strategy hasn't been properly implemented.

Is there anyone given, do you guys try and hold blood and organ, uh, accountable or push them along in the, in the right direction to make sure it is being implemented? No, I wouldn't say never seen as our role to push. Yeah. Uh, New Zealand blood and organ, we have a collaborative working relationship with 'em, but we are not, we don't manage them.

No. We are partners in trying to do a complex job. Well, Is there a role for the ministry there to do that as the sort of custodians of the health system? The min Yeah. So the, the min the ministry are the people who oversee and regulate what we do. So I would assume they're regulate and oversee all organizations that before to, Yeah. So they're the monitor, it's their job to, um, provide the monitoring of that.

Um, and it's our job to deliver the service. Um, so that's our responsibility. And when the functions that had traditionally been held in the Ministry of Health were transferred to Health New Zealand when we formed, which includes the funding for linked services. So in my team, we hold all of those transition, those contracts that transitioned over and we fund into our hospitals, all of that, um, commitment that was made at that time When that transferred across.

Thanks, Hamish. So as, as you're probably aware, this, this hearing is as a result of, um, concerns raised over over the strategy, um, I appreciate your saying that using New Zealand blood, um, services, the main is the main, um, hold of that strategy.

What role do, um, does Health New Zealand play in in that strategy? Um, and are, are you aware of the different priorities within the Strategy? We're certainly aware of six priorities within the strategy, and our role is very much on the end of when, uh, potential donors, um, are in ours, in our hands, um, opening up the sensitive conversations with their whanau, um, and trying to do that well.

Um, and also enabling the conditions whereby transplantation can proceed, so ensure that the bed is protected in intensive care. I, I was clear earlier that whilst bed capacity is absolutely not a barrier to donation mm-hmm. Um, it can sometimes mean logistically if we really are at a very tight bed situation nationally, that we slightly have to defer planned use of intensive care beds.

So major surgery sometimes require a planned bed in ICU, and so we sometimes have to defer that. So there's some logistical work that we have to do. Um, but that is our role. It is, uh, very much that. And then once we've got the consent, obviously then there's a separate team who can in and do the organ harvesting, which is another about that. So that's very much our role. Obviously we are there to collaborate if there are challenges and operational difficulties, then from the operational side, it's Rachel's team from the clinical side.

If there are questions, then we talk to our clinical counterparts, but that's where we see our role in all this. Yes. So, so of course one of the priorities is organ donation capacity. Yes. So is from the answer, can we take that actually there's no, um, capacity issue around, uh, your part of the implementation, which is, is retrieving the organs and transplanting them?

Yeah, for my conversations with my colleagues, my clinical colleagues, they're very clear that the capacity for the retrieval is not the issue. Um, if, if anywhere that we could do better, it is trying to consent more potential donations. So that's probably the area where for me, this process is encouraged us to open conversations. What would it take to have more of those conversations to consent more of the eligible pool?

Once we get to the conversation with families, it's about 44%, I think it was last year, of families consented for their loved ones organs to be used. I mean, there's a question for me about the quality of the conversations and the confidence, but this is not a time for coercion or persuasions is a time for ultimate sensitivity and cultural understanding.

Um, so I think the training side is very good, but probably having more people trained that we can be sure they're always confident and competent people around to have the conversations. But actually no, the ITU and the retrieval and the transplant capacity is not the issue. If organ organs become available, our surgeons make themselves available to ensure it happens.

A question just on, uh, I understand that people, like I do on my license, I say I'm a donor. Yeah. Um, but that information doesn't necessarily flow through if I was to pass That's correct. Is there a database or should there be a national database around who has consented to Yeah. To have their organization? Because you're probably in some quite time sensitive, um, situations too, right?

Where you need to keep the organ health thing. So the reality is that actually, um, irrespective of whether people's name is on the list, the, the, um, driver's license registry, irrespective of that, you start at the baseline of whether, whether there is yes or no on their driving license. You ask the family anyway. So that's the baseline as a new immigrant to the country, uh, I picked up my driver's license on day one of my arrival here, had my lovely conversation.

I was actually delighted to be asked about organ donation right there. And then this works. This seems pretty organized to me, but of course not everybody holds a driver's license. Um, there's a, you know, many countries in the world have independent registries. They have a merit, generally, they're costly to set up. They have to be administered, I'm sure, and and digitally linked. And so digital enablement can be a limiting factor.

I think there are probably higher priorities right now, but I'm not an expert in that. It's certainly possible. No, I could add to that one of the things that we are very, um, committed to and want to see more of in the health system is end of life planning. So end of life planning is a strategy that we see. It's critically important because it's the conversations families have before these events happen that make it easier to have the decision about donation.

Mm-hmm. So if we were to put our emphasis, um, any further rather than a registry investment and, and continuing training and opportunities for families to engage with end of life planning would have, um, a bigger impact. We know that the end of life planning includes conversations about organ good donation, and if you've agreed before you get there, um, it's much easier conversation.

Cam, um, Thank you chair. I've got a question about, um, I mean the strategy, uh, you said six points. I only could find five. Um, so Okay, sweet. That's cool. Um, one of them is empowering intensive care staff to discuss the donation. Like is Health New Zealand actually got a, have you got any, um, anything you can provide the committee to show that you've, since 2017 Health New Zealand's actually been doing this thing So well, health New Zealand's existed, But my, so I've been speaking to my intensive care colleagues,

like the heads of intensive care departments. What this is this, because this is business as usual, it is a normal part of what we talk about and what we do. And so for every, you know, obviously the pool of potential donors is very small compared to the number of people who come through IT departments. But it is a, it, it is a conversation that they have in team development, professional development.

I can't give you a bit of paper that says, um, this is, this is our target for this week or this month or this year. But it is something that is regarded as a quality activity. It is, uh, professionally actually very rewarding if you complete the process of getting, uh, somebody through the donation, uh, because it takes a lot. So it is a discussed event, uh, type situation.

But beyond that comes, so There's no, there's no sort of, uh, training support for ic? Oh, no, there is. Sorry. So we need to go through that. So there's lots of, so if you think about the role of clinical directors and chief medical people running intensive care units, this is part of their core, should we say it's their core business and business as usual, but they're extremely skilled at understand this work.

They talk to their teams. We have linked training for our nurses. So we have nurses who are trained. I think we have 97 Yep. 97, 97 at the moment, who are trained in how to have those conversations spread across the country. And we continuously train people in the importance of this conversation. I think as clinical activities go, clinicians are always looking for opportunities to improve outcomes for patients.

Um, and the patients, the person in front of you will have suffered a, a catastrophic event, but the people that you can benefit are significant. So people who are looking for donations for their patients, such as renal specialists, liver specialists, those people across the hospital, they are conversing regularly around the opportunity because we are also doing live donations. Yeah. I Was gonna ask about the deceased donation, but there's also a strategy, uh, strategy is to increase, um, the ability for hospitals to deliver deceased.

Yep. Um, how's that, is there any paper, the paper you could provide on that particular, um, think what sort of capacity increase has there been? So I think the important thing here, and Helen is Helen's outlining, is we don't have a piece of paper because it's a bit like if you asked us for a piece of paper on how we run renal dialysis services or other specialty Services, well, it would be a piece of paper on how you've increased the, uh, We could give you renal Dialysis services.

We, we could give you evidence of how we've done that. We could give you evidence of how we've grown our intensive care unit, inpatient bed capacity that has been investment over a number of years, particularly pro post COVID. It was identified, um, that there was a need for investment in intensive care and that investment was given and that has occurred. And we have more intensive care beds.

I think the really important thing that we've discovered in preparing for this and talking to our clinicians is they are very clear that when an opportunity for donation arises, that that is the number one priority for that hospital, that intensive care and that operating theater at that moment. So it's sort of, it's like a process that almost gives ups everything else because we understand, firstly, it's really good for the recipients, but it's also really good for the health system.

Uh, a person who receives a, um, a positive transplant, who has a good outcome, costs the health system less in the long run and takes a burden off other people. Um, but there are more than intensive care doctors involved in transplantations. I'll come to that. Okay. Sorry, it's just a, a quicks on something that was, earlier you talked about lack of planning, um, for older years with this, um, organ donation.

Do you see an opportunity for maybe bundling with something like EPOS and wills? Or would there be problems around that? Yeah, We have a life plan, end of life planning strategy. We have an end of life planning document that has been in the health system, particularly in the last, I think it's about eight years. There has been a concerted strategy by the Health Quality and Safety Committee to expand the role of that.

So it doesn't require enduring power of attorney, but that it's informed by that. So if someone develops the end of life plan, and if you Google, you'll find it online. I, I'm thinking just about the timing of it. 'cause what, what we've got as a responsive system. Whereas what we want is a more assertive system. Right. A proactive system. So if I can pick up one of the challenges, and I think perhaps what Rachel was referring to earlier is actually more of a public conversation about have you had a conversation with your family as opposed to more the written planning,

which we are doing a lot of work on? Yeah. For most donors, the, the people most likely to be effective donors are actually relatively well, they have to be quite well and healthy. They can't be disease ridden 'cause the organs are not suitable. Um, it has to be a sudden death for solid organ donation, not tissue donation. I know that's not what we're here to talk about, tissue donation, but eyes and velps and so on.

Um, but actually it's in a sense, it's, it's the unfit people doing sporting activities. It's the people who have a sudden catastrophic, unexpected stroke when they're otherwise young un fitt. And those are often not the people for whom end of life planning is being considered in a formal sense. So it's the talk to your family. If something happened to you when you walk out here today, what would you want to happen to your organs?

I know what I would want, but Yeah. Yeah. I does that, does that help? Yeah. About that? Yeah. A bit of frustration in the sense that we had blood and blood and organ service and, and, and now we've got you and we, you're saying that the strategy implementation is not your responsibility at all? No. Is that, that's Health New Zealand's position?

Well, our position is that we are here to deliver on the strategy and that's what we do. But we're not here to run the strategy Is with blood and organ service. Okay. How much you, Part of the strategy was improved training? We have kind of touched on that a little bit. Yeah. Um, and in, in the original strategy document, um, it sites some work done by ER and young in 2016 that between 30 and 50% of, um, intensive care physicians have had some sort of training.

Has that number changed? Um, since, since the strategy was released? I don't think we know the answer to that question. Yeah. Um, we didn't know the question had been asked, but we can provide that information. I, and I'm certain it will have Yeah, I mean, so, so we can't give you x percentage of why percentage young dun because we haven't asked that question.

What I do know is that since Health New Zealand is a single entity as opposed to 20 distinct different health boards Yeah. Um, that actually there is a much more unification and standardization of approaches. And so things like, like the link nurses who are trained by NZ blood and organ, but who are within our units, um, the, these conversations do happen.

So I think you'll probably find awareness is significantly higher. But this culture of business as usual organ donation is everybody's business in that environment, uh, I would hope is highly pervasive. Um, so it'd be interesting if the EXOS were repeated. I'm not suggesting we spend the money to do it again. Uh, but I can sort of give you my personal assurance that by this process happening and me asking some questions that we are raising the profile and the having the conversation, which I think will turn out to be a positive thing.

Um, but we will see. Mm-hmm. I mean, do you suppose, sorry. I suppose the ultimate aim is to have greater number Yes. Of organ, organ trans transplantation. So what measures are you putting in place to ensure that happens? Um, I appreciate there's been, you've mentioned a number of things, but are we actually seeing that dial shift?

I think there's A couple of things. The percentage of dinners has gone up in the last year or so. As we, we saw a 9% increase last year, 12% in, in tissue increase. I can't tell you whether that is a definite trend or not. Um, but certainly post pandemic because we have the dip, uh, the global effect, uh, post pandemic where everything drop and how much is natural recovery and how much is due to activity.

We can't be sure. But sincerely hope that as we are improving to be a more holistic organization, that standardization will improve. Personally, I'd like to see a greater proportion of our nurses trained the link nurse route. I think we, I think we feel that would be constructive thing and we're hoping to explore that. And as I said, I will be encouraging all the departments to have a formal conversation about this, which I think in turn will also help.

We're open to other suggestions. We collaborate with NZ blood and organ, um, and we're open to conversation with them about what other things we could be doing. Um, actually, yeah, There are some interesting facts when, when you look at the information that's been supplied to you as well, where the number of families who say no. Yeah. And that is probably the place where there is the greatest opportunity because there are families who've had the events, how you get people to say yes.

Right. That is also about public promotion. Um, that is about the strategy that's being driven from the Ministry of Health at the New Zealand Organ Blood Service. It's not our responsibility to drive that public conversation. We do implement, um, end of life planning and end of life planning is for everyone. I have an end of life plan. I'm a perfectly fit, healthy person, um, which says, this is what I want to happen.

And my family know that those conversations are really important. Um, I think those are the conversations that need to be had with the Ministry of Health and the New Zealand. I'll go Limited On time. Yes. Yeah, Just the operational questions. Uh, we heard that the, the, I think the family members can actually over, over ruining the correct the wishes.

So under the system, what percentage of registered organs donors actually go on to donate their organs after death? Yes. It's so, so of those who are asked where the conversation starts in around 200 a year, um, and of those who asked about 90 go on to the, to be the next stage.

And actually once the process begins, actually only 70 end up being eligible because those, so that, those are the last years' figures. So back 290, 70, those, there will have been 20 where the process was started. And once we started to explore further, we discovered additional disease that we didn't know previously. And we rented them ineligible. But the, the gap is then from the approximately 600 that might have been eligible to, to start the process, that 200 to 90.

So about 44% of those who, um, just are whether, so basically there's that 56% gap where the families are saying no 50 50 and understanding that is important. That went, it takes a lot of cultural sensitivity. Our Counter, Greg And I was just, so one thing nobody's very Short on time Has been just about, um, opt in.

Um, we have an opt-in system here to organ donation. I come from a country which moved to opt, opt to opta. Sorry, go ahead. Just one question. Um, personal situation. We've encountered, people often think that organ donation and donating the body are the same thing, are very different with organ donation. You get the, you still get the body absolutely. Back for burial or way Very rapidly. Do you find that that is actually what actually often families think that they're not gonna get a body if they get an organization? See,

You start sometimes, one of the questions that people do ask quite quickly and understanding death rights and rituals of different cultures, different religions is vitally important part of the process and the training. So that ultimate sensitivity and real cultural awareness is important. But actually those things are quite easily reassured and explained. And, you know, all major religions, all cultural groups don't, don't have opposition.

Sorry. I'll jump in there. Oh, sorry Jen. Sorry. Just a quick stop on that. Um, uh, in, in terms of, um, Molly for example, very hesitant to donate organs when they do often they ask when that recipient, um, then passes away that that organ is removed and sent back to the family to Be buried with the Yeah.

How much is that, are you seeing of that and what sort of percentage? Well, interesting. Can I asked a lot of question 'cause I'd heard some myths about this sort of marry reticence to donate and actually the findings, it's not actually true once you get past the initial conversation. So, uh, Maori families are just as likely as any other to agree to donation.

I think those conversations happen. I can't give the answer wasn't, that Wasn't meaning that I was meaning I was asking the return, the return Of the, I don't have that data. Actually, I haven't asked that question and I don't think that is something that we've seen or that we're aware of. So I don't think, and that is something we would jump, not be able to agree to Doesn't To the initial We're running over time.

So I'll just pull back, um, sorry. There may be a couple more questions. I'm sorry we didn't get to them. That's okay. We're over top. Thank you. Thank you very much for coming in today. Appreciate. Thank you.
Sorry. Sorry. There's note back to private.

The petitions isn't good. You're taking out petitions. See, good to see where they, yeah. Someone From petitions committee keeps sending them over here. Couple hospital passes, hospital passes to the health committee, you know, mean and then you read into the front row. We're on. We're on. No, great. Well welcome Kathleen.

Very good to have you here. Um, firstly, uh, welcome. Secondly, that is a wonderful oh, way have plenty steamers jersey in the weekend. Yes. And I hope I'll be there at domain. Come on the, it'll just be Canterbury this week. That's not the reason we're talking with you, Kathleen. We've got you in here around, um, obstetrics specialists for maternity services at WHA hospital.

Uh, we have you for 10 minutes. I'll open it up to you. You may use that time as you wish. If you want to give us, um, some time for questions, you may. But I'll hand that time to you now and welcome. Um, and thank you for having me. Um, I'm a bit out of my depth here 'cause I'm just, just a mum in the community over here and I'm just someone that sort of cares about what's happening in our local hospital.

And it kind of spiraled a little bit because I've got a friend who, um, pushes me to do things like start petitions and things. So friend. Yeah, I know, right? We all need someone. Um, so I, I guess I'm just here today to sort of, um, to encourage you to take seriously the views of these 5,000 odd people that signed the petition.

It's a really big number for a small community like, um, Whakatane, and I think it really showed how very important this matter is to our community. As time has gone on since we first found out about the maternity services being, um, downgraded.

Uh, we, we haven't sort of found that things have settled or that things are getting better or anything like that. We know that they're working behind the scenes to get new obstetricians in. But all of the, uh, the information that we're receiving is that it's still gonna be next year before any of that actually lands on the ground here in Whakatane.

That we're still gonna be at the current status quo here in Whakatane. And the current status quo is not good enough. We have mums being transported by ambulance, by air to a hospital that is hours and hours away from their home, from their families. We have, um, babies put in danger and we even lost a baby, baby Navy who died.

And that has a huge impact on our community and we feel, we feel a great, I think a weight of grief for what our moms are experiencing. The other thing that goes, goes, that has come from this is that as we go on, we, we have discovered that the impact of this change in our hospital is wider than just moms and babies.

We have, um, older women in our community going through menopause and perimenopause or other gynecological stages of their life who are unable to access timely treatment for even, um, conditions that impact on their lives in a very, very, um, severe manner.

I know one woman who has been waiting for months for an appointment who really desperately needs a hysterectomy because she has a mass in her uterus and she just can't even get on the list. And that's, we are told by talking to the, um, GY gynecologists and, um, the pe the specialists that we are able to access.

We hear that that is because of the pressure that is being put on our local system by the loss of those, um, in, in, um, the maternity, when the maternity system here. So the, the flow on effects for our community have been just devastating really, because we, um, it's so many more people than we originally thought who are being impacted.

And the worry is that the longer we go to getting things sorted, the more those timelines get pushed out and the longer it's gonna take to claw back the waiting lists and all of that. So, um, I guess I'm here to say that this is still very much, um, an issue that is present and ongoing in our community.

It hasn't been resolved and we really need to see things getting, um, pushed to getting resolved. Um, the second part of that is that, um, I guess our petition and the submission that I made is that we really want to see a review done of what has happened to get our hospital into this situation and, um, and how to ensure that it doesn't happen again.

And we really, really want that to be, um, that needs to be an external review. I think the community has lost faith because the communication that has come from TU has been lacking in so many ways. Um, it has been, um, that not timely communication.

They are very slow. They didn't even announce this change until it was just about to happen. That's right. And uh, so I think that if a review is done of what has gone into the maternity gone on in the maternity ring to cause this problem and, um, and what needs to be done next time to stop that happening, um, we, we really need to see that, I think done by an external group to make sure that the community can have faith in the recommendations and, and in the review.

We also, we also, um, if, um, I think because the situation that we have at the moment is not okay and it's ongoing in its current, um, in, in our community that things are not going well for us. Um, we need some solutions to, um, improve the services in the maternity wing at Whakatane Hospital right now.

Thank you. Um, we have a few members with hands up. I'll go with Ingrid first and then I see Johanna as Well. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, thank you Kathleen for your mahi and just acknowledging to the families who have lost a baby and those women who are waiting with some very concerning, um, concerning symptoms, uh, both Hu Linda and I were there at the rally and we could feel the strength of concern from the community.

So just wanna acknowledge that as well and the community for rallying together. Um, you have mentioned the poor communication from Health New Zealand. I'm wondering what responses you've had from the Minister of Health and, uh, whether you, uh, feel that this issue and women's health in particular in Whakatane is being taken seriously enough.

No, we haven't. Um, to my knowledge, I don't think the minister has been, um, to Whakatane to, um, visit the hospital to find it, to talk to our community, to find out anything about what's going on on a personal level. And I think that's, um, to us that reflects on, reflects poorly because we, we feel as a, a sort of semi-rural community, a regional community that, um, that everything happens in the cities and everything that that goes on, it matters what happens in toga, but we don't matter.

And I think that's a really poor message for our community to receive that. Do, Do you think, thank you for that. Do you think it would be helpful if he heard this, if he did come down to see you? Would that be a a, something that would help the situation? Yeah, Absolutely. I think that the community would feel heard per perhaps, perhaps, and, you know, hopefully he would hear us and um, and actually push, push for us as well because we really need some action here.

Kathleen. I can, I can liaise with the minister's office on that as well. And you know, as the member for Toga, um, I'm fairly close to Whakatane, um, and as well as as chair of the health committee. Um, so we, we can, we can make sure that there is, um, some insights going there and we can, we can have further conversations with you.

Thank you. So, um, come back to you on that one. Um, I saw HOA had her hand up. Hoa. Yeah. Thanks. Bye Kathleen. And thanks for bringing the continued concerns of the and wa Western Plenty, um, whanau concerns to the Health Select committee, as we've heard very clearly from you and others that the state of play has not changed.

Uh, so the PI order Healthy Features Act, uh, 2022, um, established Health New Zealand and requires Health New Zealand to ensure that Maori and other population groups have equitable access to the health services they need.

We looked at how effectively Health New Zealand is supporting more equitable access to planned care treatment through the use of nationally consistent processes to patient prioritization. Essentially, we wanted to know what Health New Zealand is doing to ensure the people with similar health needs have similar access to the treatment that they need. We focused on planned care treatments, so sometimes those are called elective arranged or scheduled treatments.

They're an important part of the health system and include things like operations to remove cataracts or tonsils placed joints, hips, knees, ankles, repair hernias, that kind of thing. And there are hundreds of thousands of planned care interventions performed each year to support the health and wellbeing of New Zealanders. We looked at things like the support and guidance available to staff, how Health New Zealand aligns supporting processes such as those for booking patients and managing waiting lists.

Uh, we looked at monitoring and evaluation of the variations in access to planned care and what Action Health New Zealand takes to fix any problems that they identify. Richard will now talk a bit about the key findings of our work. Thank you, Lee. Uh, new Zealanders do not have the same access to planned care treatments. A person's ability to access the treatments they need and or acquire very significantly depending on where they live.

Uh, while people living in rural areas, those experiencing social deprivation, Maori Pacific peoples and disabled people are disproportionately waiting longer for treatment. Health. New Zealand is working to introduce nation nationally consistent thresholds for access to treatment. Uh, the district's specific thresholds still in place are obscuring the extent of unmet need for treatment.

In some districts, significant work could be required to deal with the changes in demand that could result from the introduction of national consistent thresholds across specialties. Uh, health New Zealand has been working to improve the timeliness of treatment. This includes ensuring that people are appropriately prioritized for treatment and receive treatment in priority order. However, equity is not always a central consideration of health New Zealand's efforts in this space.

There is a risk that some efforts designed to provide more timely treatment for New Zealanders like outsourcing could result in less equitable access to treatment. Health New Zealand has made considerable progress in monitoring and reporting on how well it is providing planned care across the country. But there are significant gaps in the information it collects, and including levels of unmet need for treatment and the information about disabled people's access to planned care.

Health New Zealand needs this information to better understand and remove inequities and access. And we made four recommendations focused on ensuring that equity, equity, sorry, of access is central to the wider improvements in providing treatment. Uh, this includes preparing a plan and with a clear timeline for the introduction of nationally consistent thresholds for treatment.

Uh, ensure that it has clear actions to improve equity of access to treatment, and that efforts to improve timeliness, do not increase inequities, strengthen its knowledge of equitable access to treatment by filling gaps in information that collects and improving the use of that information and preparing a suite of planned care measures to enable clear public reporting about access to planned care. Over you, Karen.

Thank you, Richard. Um, our suggested next steps. Um, we suggest that the committee should call Health New Zealand in to understand what progress it has made in supporting more equitable access.
